body_text:
Section 5.4 Products of Rational and Trigonometric Functions

and because of the problem at $x=a$. The first integral's principal value is
$$
\text { P.V. } \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\sin x}{x-a} d x=\lim _{\substack{r \rightarrow 0^{+} \\ R \rightarrow \infty}}\left(\int_{-R}^{a-r} \frac{\sin x}{x-a} d x+\int_{a+r}^{R} \frac{\sin x}{x-a} d x\right)
$$
(Figure 11 represents the case $a>0$ ). Consider the integral of $f(z)=\frac{e^{i x}}{z-a}$ o the closed contour $\gamma_{r, R}$ in Figure 12, where we have indented the contour arou $x=a$. The larger semi-circle $\sigma_{R}$ has radius $R$ and positive orientation. The smal semi-circle has radius $r$; it is negatively oriented and we shall call it $-\sigma_{r}$. Sir $f(z)$ is analytic on and inside $\gamma_{r, R}$, Cauchy's theorem implies that
$$
\begin{aligned}
0 & =\int_{-R}^{a-r} \frac{e^{i x}}{x-a} d x+\int_{-\sigma_{r}} \frac{e^{i z}}{z-a} d z+\int_{a+r}^{R} \frac{e^{i x}}{x-a} d z+\int_{\sigma_{R}} \frac{e^{i z}}{z-a} d z \\
& =I_{1}+I_{2}+I_{3}+I_{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

By Jordan's lemma, $I_{4} \rightarrow 0$ as $R \rightarrow \infty$. By the shrinking path lemma, $I_{2} \rightarrow-i \pi$ as $r \rightarrow 0$ (note the negative sign due to the fact that the path of integration negatively oriented). Thus

con-
$$
\lim _{\substack{r \rightarrow 0 \\ R \rightarrow \infty}}\left(\int_{-R}^{a-r} \frac{e^{i x}}{x-a} d x+\int_{a+r}^{R} \frac{e^{i x}}{x-a} d x\right)=i \pi e^{i a}=\pi(-\sin a+i \cos a)
$$
and the desired integral identities follow upon taking real and imaginary parts.
Exercises 5.4
In Exercises 1-10, evaluate the given improper integral. Make sure to outline steps in your solutions. Carry out the details of the solution even if the integ follows from general formulas that we derived previously.
1. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\cos 4 x}{x^{2}+1} d x=\pi e^{-4}$.
2. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\sin \frac{\pi x}{2}}{x^{2}+2 x+4} d x=-\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}} e^{-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \pi}$.
3. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x \sin 3 x}{x^{2}+2} d x=e^{-3 \sqrt{2} \pi}$.
4. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\sin \pi x}{x^{2}+2 x+4} d x=0$.
5. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x^{2} \cos 2 x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)^{2}} d x=-\frac{\pi}{2} e^{-2}$.
6. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\cos x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)^{2}} d x=\frac{\pi}{e}$.
7. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\cos (a(x-b))}{x^{2}+c^{2}} d x=\frac{\pi}{c} e^{-|a| c} \cos (a b) \quad(a$ real, $c>0)$.
8. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\cos (4 \pi x)}{2 x^{2}+x+1} d x=-2 e^{-\sqrt{2} \pi} \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{7}}$.
9. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x \cos \pi x}{x^{2}+x+9} d x=\pi e^{-\frac{\sqrt{35}}{2} \pi}$.
10. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\sin \pi x}{x^{2}+x+1} d x=-\frac{2 \pi}{\sqrt{3}} e^{-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \pi}$.
